We must judge ourselves and evaluate our own actions, but we should not force our beliefs on everyone. We must not judge rashly or pass judgment on our brother without good reason. We must not assume the worst of people. Here is a fitting rebuke to those who quarrel with their brethren for minor faults while allowing themselves to commit greater ones. Some sins are like small pieces of dust, while others are like large beams; some are like a gnat, others like a camel. It is true that there is no sin too small; if it is a piece of dust, it is in the eye; if it is a gnat, it is in the throat—both are painful and dangerous, and we cannot be comfortable or well until they are removed. What charity teaches us to call a splinter in our brother's eye, true repentance and godly sorrow will teach us to call a beam in our own. It is as strange that a person can be in a sinful, miserable condition without realizing it, as it is for someone to have a beam in their eye and not notice it; but the god of this world blinds their minds. Here is a good rule for those who correct others: first reform yourself.

Prayer is the appointed way to obtain what we need. Pray; pray often; make prayer a priority, and be serious and earnest about it. Ask as a beggar asks for alms. Ask as a traveler asks for directions. Seek as if you have lost something valuable or as a merchant seeks precious pearls. Knock as someone who wants to enter a house knocks at the door. Sin has shut and barred the door against us; by prayer we knock. Whatever you pray for, according to the promise, will be given to you if God sees fit, and what more could you want? This applies to all who pray correctly; everyone who asks receives, whether Jew or Gentile, young or old, rich or poor, high or low, master or servant, learned or unlearned—all are equally welcome at the throne of grace if they come in faith. It is explained by a comparison taken from earthly parents and their willingness to give their children what they ask. Parents are often overly fond, but God is all-wise; he knows what we need, what we desire, and what is best for us. Let us never suppose our heavenly Father would tell us to pray and then refuse to hear or give us what would be harmful.

Christ came to teach us not only what we are to know and believe but also what we are to do; not only toward God but toward people; not only toward those of our own group, but toward all people with whom we have to deal. We must do to our neighbor what we acknowledge as fitting and reasonable. In our dealings with others, we must put ourselves in their situation and act accordingly. There are only two ways: right and wrong, good and evil, the way to heaven and the way to hell; everyone is walking one or the other—there is no middle place hereafter, no middle way now. All people are either saints or sinners, godly or ungodly. Consider what happens on the way of sin and sinners: the gate is wide and stands open. You may enter this gate with all your desires about you; it does not restrain appetites or passions. It is a broad way; there are many paths in it—a choice of sinful ways. There is a large group traveling this way. But what benefit is there in going to hell with others simply because they will not go to heaven with us? The way to eternal life is narrow. We are not in heaven as soon as we pass through the narrow gate. Self must be denied, the body kept under control, and corruptions overcome. Daily temptations must be resisted; duties must be performed. We must watch carefully in all things and walk cautiously; and we must endure much suffering. Yet this way should invite us all—it leads to life: to present comfort in God’s favor, which is the life of the soul; to eternal bliss, the hope of which at the end of our journey will make all the difficulties along the road seem easy. This clear declaration of Christ has been disregarded by many who have tried to twist its meaning; but throughout history, true followers of Christ have always been seen as unusual and unfashionable—and those who side with the majority have gone on down the broad road to destruction. If we want to serve God, we must be firm in our faith. Can we often hear about the narrow gate and the difficult way and how few find it without feeling troubled for ourselves or considering whether we are on the narrow path and what progress we are making?

Nothing prevents people so much from entering the narrow gate and becoming true followers of Christ as the worldly, comforting, flattering teachings of those who oppose the truth. They can be identified by the direction and effects of their teachings. Some of their behavior and character is contrary to the mind of Christ. Those opinions do not come from God that lead to sin.

Christ here shows that it will not be enough to acknowledge him as our Master only in words. It is necessary for our happiness that we believe in Christ, that we repent of sin, that we live a holy life, and that we love one another. This is his will—our sanctification. Let us beware of resting in outward privileges and actions, lest we deceive ourselves and perish eternally, as many do with a lie in their right hand. Everyone who calls the name of Christ must turn away from all sin. There are others whose religion rests only on hearing; it goes no further; their minds are filled with empty ideas. These two kinds of listeners are like two builders. This parable teaches us to hear and do the sayings of the Lord Jesus—some may seem difficult to accept, but they must be done. Christ is laid as a foundation, and everything else besides Christ is sand. Some build their hopes on worldly prosperity; others on an outward profession of religion. They risk everything on these things, but they are all sand—too weak to support such hopes of heaven. A storm is coming that will test everyone’s work. When God takes away a person's soul, where is the hope of the hypocrite? The house fell in the storm when the builder needed it most and expected it would be a shelter. It fell before he could build another. May the Lord make us wise builders for eternity. Then nothing will separate us from the love of Christ Jesus. The crowds were amazed at the wisdom and power of Christ’s teachings—and this sermon, no matter how often read, is always new. Every word proves its Author to be Divine. Let us be more determined and earnest, making some one or other of these blessings and Christian virtues the main focus of our thoughts, even for weeks together. Let us not rest in general and vague desires after them, whereby we grasp at all but catch nothing.
